paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'The way of nutritive qi (yingqi) is such that grain intake is its treasure. Grain enters the stomach and is thereupon transmitted to the lung; it overflows within and disperses without, and its refined and concentrated portion travels through the channel passageways, circulating constantly without cease, ending and beginning again — this is called the guiding thread of Heaven and Earth. Thus the qi emerges from the Taiyin and pours into the Hand Yangming; it ascends and pours into the Foot Yangming, descends to the instep, and pours into the cleft between the great toes, there joining with the Taiyin. It then ascends to reach the thigh, and from the spleen pours into the heart. Following the Hand Shaoyin, it emerges at the armpit, descends the arm, and pours into the little finger, joining with the Hand Taiyang. It ascends, rides over the armpit, emerges within the cheek beneath the eye, pours into the inner canthus, ascends to the vertex, descends the nape, and joins with the Foot Taiyang. It follows the spine, descends to the sacrum, descends further to pour into the tip of the little toe, follows the sole of the foot, and pours into the Foot Shaoyin. It ascends and pours into the kidney, and from the kidney pours outward into the heart, dispersing through the chest. Following the Pericardium vessel [Hand Jueyin], it emerges at the armpit, descends the arm, passes between the two sinews, enters the palm, emerges at the tip of the middle finger, then returns to pour into the tip of the ring finger, joining with the Hand Shaoyang. It ascends and pours into danzhong (the Center of the Chest), disperses through the triple burner, and from the triple burner pours into the gallbladder, emerges at the rib-side, and pours into the Foot Shaoyang. It descends to the instep, and once again from the instep pours into the cleft between the great toes, joining with the Foot Jueyin. It ascends to the liver, and from the liver rises to pour into the lung, ascends along the throat, enters the openings of the nasopharynx, and terminates at the nostril gate (xumen). Its branching offshoot ascends the forehead, follows the vertex, descends the middle of the nape, follows the spine and enters the sacrum — this is the Governing Vessel (Du Mai). It sends a network to the genitals, ascends past the pubic hair, enters the navel, rises along the interior of the abdomen, enters the Empty Basin (quepen), descends to pour into the lung, and once more emerges at the Taiyin. This is the course traveled by nutritive qi, the constant order of its forward and reverse flow.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear about the measurements of the vessels.' Qibo replied: 'The six yang channels of the hand run from the hand to the head, a length of five chi (a chi being roughly a Chinese "foot"); five sixes make three zhang (a zhang being ten chi). The six yin channels of the hand run from the hand to the chest, three chi five cun (a cun being roughly a Chinese "inch"); three sixes make one zhang eight chi, and five sixes make three chi, together two zhang one chi. The six yang channels of the foot run from the foot up to the head, eight chi; six eights make four zhang eight chi. The six yin channels of the foot run from the foot to the chest, six chi five cun; six sixes make three zhang six chi, and five sixes make three chi, together three zhang nine chi. The qiaomai (Springing Vessel) runs from the foot to the eye, seven chi five cun; two sevens make one zhang four chi, and two fives make one chi, together one zhang five chi. The Governing Vessel (Du Mai) and the Conception Vessel (Ren Mai) are each four chi five cun; two fours make eight chi, and two fives make one chi, together nine chi. In all, the total sum is sixteen zhang two chi — this is the great thoroughfare of the channel passageways through which qi travels.'

The channels lie in the interior; their branching, transverse offshoots are the network vessels; and the further branches of the network vessels are the grandchild vessels (sunluo). Where fullness and blood-stasis occur, one should swiftly eliminate them by needling; where there is excess, one should drain it; where there is deficiency, one should supplement it by administering medicinal decoctions. The five viscera constantly make themselves manifest above, at the seven orifices. Thus the lung qi communicates with the nose, and when the lung is harmonious, the nose is able to distinguish foul and fragrant odors. The heart qi communicates with the tongue, and when the heart is harmonious, the tongue is able to distinguish the five flavors. The liver qi communicates with the eyes, and when the liver is harmonious, the eyes are able to distinguish the five colors. The spleen qi communicates with the mouth, and when the spleen is harmonious, the mouth is able to distinguish the five grains. The kidney qi communicates with the ears, and when the kidney is harmonious, the ears are able to hear the five notes. When the five viscera are not harmonious, the seven orifices fail to communicate; when the six bowels are not in accord, [qi and fluids] linger and form welling-abscesses. Thus, when evil qi resides in the bowels, the yang vessels become disharmonious; when the yang vessels are disharmonious, qi lingers there, and when qi lingers there, the yang qi becomes excessive. When yang qi becomes excessively full, the yin fails to flow freely; when the yin vessels fail to flow freely, blood lingers there, and when blood lingers there, the yin qi becomes excessive. When yin qi becomes excessively full, the yang qi can no longer nourish it — this is called 'closure' (guan). When yang qi becomes excessively full, the yin qi can no longer nourish it — this is called 'blockage' (ge). When yin and yang are both excessively full and can no longer nourish each other, this is called 'closure and blockage' (guange). One who suffers closure and blockage will die before living out his allotted years.

Huangdi said: 'Where does the qiaomai arise and where does it terminate, and what qi nourishes it?' Qibo replied: 'The qiaomai is a branch of the Shaoyin; it arises behind Rangu (Blazing Valley) point. It ascends above the medial malleolus, rises directly along the inner thigh, enters the genital region, ascends along the interior of the chest, enters the Empty Basin, emerges above in front of Renying (Man's Prognosis) point, enters the cheek, attaches to the inner canthus of the eye, and joins with the Taiyang; together with the yang qiaomai it ascends, and when the qi converges and returns, it provides moisture — when the qi that nourishes the eyes fails to arrive, the eyes will not close [in sleep].'

Huangdi said: 'Why does qi travel through the five viscera alone and not nourish the six bowels?' Qibo replied: 'It is not that qi fails to travel [to the bowels] — it flows like water, like the sun and moon in their motion, without ceasing. Thus the yin vessels nourish the viscera, and the yang vessels nourish the bowels, circling like a ring without end, so that none can discern where it begins; it ends and begins again. The overflowing qi that spills forth irrigates the viscera and bowels within, and moistens the interstices of the flesh without.'

Huangdi said: 'The qiaomai has both a yin and a yang branch — which vessel should be counted in the reckoning?' Qibo said: 'In counting for a man, one counts his yang [qiaomai]; in counting for a woman, one counts her yin [qiaomai]. The one that is counted is regarded as the [primary] channel, while the one not counted is regarded as a network vessel.'

title_en: The Generation and Meeting of Nutritive and Defensive Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'From where does a person receive qi? Where do yin and yang meet? What qi becomes nutritive qi, and what qi becomes defensive qi? Whence is nutritive qi generated, and where does defensive qi converge? The qi of the old differs from that of the vigorous, and yin and yang occupy different positions — I wish to hear of their meeting.' Qibo replied: 'A person receives qi from grain. Grain enters the stomach and is transmitted to the lung, and the five viscera and six bowels all receive qi from it. Its clear portion becomes nutritive qi, and its turbid portion becomes defensive qi. Nutritive qi travels within the vessels, and defensive qi travels outside the vessels. Nutritive qi circulates ceaselessly, and after fifty circuits it once again meets in a great convergence; yin and yang are strung through one another like a ring without end. Defensive qi travels twenty-five circuits through the yin and twenty-five circuits through the yang, dividing into day and night; thus when the qi reaches yang it rouses one to wake, and when it reaches yin it brings one to rest. Hence it is said that at midday yang is at its height, and this is called double yang; at midnight yin is at its height, and this is called double yin. Thus the Taiyin governs the interior and the Taiyang governs the exterior, each traveling twenty-five circuits, dividing into day and night. At midnight yin is at its height; after midnight yin begins to decline, and at dawn, when yin is exhausted, yang receives the qi. At midday yang is at its height; as the sun moves westward yang declines, and at sunset, when yang is exhausted, yin receives the qi. At midnight the great convergence occurs, when the myriad people all lie asleep — this is called the closing of yin. At dawn, when yin is exhausted, yang receives the qi once more, and so it continues without cease, following the same order as Heaven and Earth.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes old people to be unable to sleep soundly at night? What qi causes the young and vigorous to be unable to sleep during the day?' Qibo replied: 'In the vigorous, qi and blood are abundant, the flesh is smooth and supple, and the pathways of qi are open and unobstructed, so that the movement of nutritive and defensive qi does not lose its normal course; hence they are alert by day and sleep soundly by night. In the aged, qi and blood have declined, the flesh has withered, and the pathways of qi have become rough and impeded; the qi of the five viscera contends within, the nutritive qi has grown scant and weak, while the defensive qi assails the interior — hence they are not alert by day and cannot sleep soundly by night.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear by what pathway nutritive and defensive qi travel, and whence they come.' Qibo replied: 'Nutritive qi emerges from the middle burner, and defensive qi emerges from the lower burner.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear whence the triple burner emerges.' Qibo replied: 'The upper burner emerges from the upper opening of the stomach; it runs alongside the throat and ascends, passes through the diaphragm, and spreads through the chest; it courses to the armpit, follows the division of the Taiyin, and travels along, then returns to reach the Yangming; it ascends to the tongue and descends to the Foot Yangming. It constantly travels together with nutritive qi twenty-five circuits through yang and twenty-five circuits through yin, completing one full cycle; thus after fifty circuits it once again meets in the great convergence at the Hand Taiyin.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person has heat, and food and drink descend into the stomach before its qi has become settled, sweat will issue forth — sometimes from the face, sometimes from the back, sometimes from half the body — failing to follow the pathway of defensive qi as it emerges. Why is this?' Qibo said: 'This occurs when one is injured by wind from without, so that the interstices of the flesh open within; the body hair steams and the interstices leak, and defensive qi rushes toward the opening, and so cannot follow its proper pathway. This qi is swift, fierce, slippery, and rapid — wherever an opening appears, it escapes through it, and so cannot follow its regular course. This is called "leakage" (louxie).'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear whence the middle burner emerges.' Qibo replied: 'The middle burner likewise lies alongside the stomach, emerging just after the upper burner. What it receives of qi, it separates out the waste and dregs, steams the body fluids, and transforms their refined essence, sending it upward to pour into the lung vessel, where it is transformed into blood, in order to nourish and sustain the body. Nothing is more precious than this; hence it alone is able to travel through the channel passageways, and it is called nutritive qi.'

Huangdi said: 'Now, blood and qi have different names yet belong to the same category — what does this mean?' Qibo replied: 'Nutritive and defensive qi are the essential qi; blood is the spirit qi. Hence blood and qi, though differently named, belong to the same category. Thus one who has lost blood should not be made to sweat, and one who has lost sweat should not be depleted of blood; hence in human life there are two ways to die from their loss, but no way to gain twice from preserving them.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear whence the lower burner emerges.' Qibo replied: 'The lower burner branches off at the coiled intestine, pours into the bladder, and seeps into it. Water and grain ordinarily reside together within the stomach; they form waste and dregs, which together descend into the large intestine and become the lower burner, seeping downward together. The clear fluid is filtered and separated out, and follows the lower burner to seep into the bladder.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person drinks wine, the wine also enters the stomach; yet even before the grain has been digested, the urine is passed first — why is this?' Qibo replied: 'Wine is the fluid of digested grain. Its qi is fierce and clear; hence, although it enters after the grain, its fluid is discharged before the grain's.' Huangdi said: 'Excellent. I have heard it said that the upper burner is like a mist, the middle burner is like a froth, and the lower burner is like a drainage ditch — this is precisely what is meant.'

title_en: The Qi of the Four Seasons
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'The qi of the four seasons each takes a different form, and the hundred diseases that arise each have their own origin — in moxibustion and needling, what is the fixed rule?' Qibo replied: 'The qi of the four seasons each has its own location, and the fixed rule of moxibustion and needling is to obtain the proper qi-point (qixue, acupoint). Thus in spring one selects the channels, the blood vessels, and the spaces between the flesh divisions — in severe cases, one needles deeply; in milder cases, one needles shallowly. In summer one selects the flourishing channels and the grandchild network vessels, needling at the flesh divisions so as to sever [the pathogen] from the skin. In autumn one selects the channel transport points (jingshu); when evil qi resides in the bowels, one selects the He-Sea point. In winter one selects the Well and Spring points, and must needle deeply and retain the needle.'

For warm malaria with no sweating, apply fifty-nine punctures; for wind-water swelling with distension of the skin, apply fifty-seven punctures. Wherever blood appears in the skin, remove all of it by needling. For diarrhea with undigested food, supplement above the three yin channels of the leg, supplementing Yinlingquan (Yin Mound Spring); in all these cases retain the needle for a long time, until the heat has dispersed, and then stop.

For cramping of the sinews on the yang aspect, treat the yang; for cramping of the sinews on the yin aspect, treat the yin — in both cases needle promptly. For plain water-swelling (edema without other complication), first select the point three cun below Huangu (Ring Valley), and needle it with the pi needle (a flat, blade-like needle); having needled, insert a tube [to drain the fluid], and let it flow in and be drawn out repeatedly, so as to exhaust the swelling fluid entirely — the abdomen must become firm. If the fluid drains slowly, the patient will feel vexed and oppressed; if it drains rapidly, the patient will remain calm. Needle once every other day until the swelling fluid is exhausted, and then stop. Give the patient a fluid-restricting decoction; at the time of needling, let the patient drink only this, without eating; when eating, allow no drinking; and permit no other food — this regimen continues for one hundred and thirty-five days.

For fixed impediment (zhuobi) that does not resolve, and chronic cold that does not abate, promptly select Zusanli (Leg Three Li); when it reaches the bone, the bone becomes withered and dry. For irregularity in the bowels, select Zusanli — if replete, drain it; if deficient, supplement it. For wind-malignity (leprosy-wind, lifeng), needle directly over the swelling as a matter of course. After needling, use a sharp needle to needle the site again, pressing to expel the malign qi, and stop only when the swelling is entirely gone. Let the patient eat only the prescribed diet, and no other food.

When the abdomen constantly rumbles and qi surges up to strike the chest, causing panting so severe that one cannot stand for long, the evil qi resides in the large intestine — needle the Huang Yuan (Source point of the Vitals), Shangjuxu (Upper Great Hollow), and Zusanli. When the lower abdomen pulls upon the testicles, drawing on the lower back and spine, and surges up to strike the heart, the evil qi resides in the small intestine, connecting with the system of the testicles, attaching to the spine, penetrating the liver and lung, and linking with the system of the heart. When this qi becomes excessive, reversal occurs, surging upward to strike the intestines and stomach, steaming the liver, dispersing into the huang (the region between the heart and diaphragm), and knotting at the navel. Hence one selects Huang Yuan to disperse it, needles the Taiyin to supply it, selects the Jueyin to bring it downward, selects Xiajuxu (Lower Great Hollow) to eliminate it, and presses along the channel through which it has passed in order to regulate it.

When a person is prone to vomiting, vomits bitter fluid, sighs deeply and repeatedly, feels a fluttering unease within the heart, and fears as though someone were about to seize him — the evil qi resides in the gallbladder, and the rebellion occurs in the stomach. When gallbladder fluid leaks out, the mouth becomes bitter; when stomach qi rebels, one vomits bitter fluid — hence this is called 'vomiting gallbladder [fluid].' Select Zusanli to bring the qi downward. If stomach qi rebels, needle the blood network vessels of the Shaoyang to close off the gallbladder's rebellion, and then regulate its deficiency and excess so as to eliminate the evil qi.

When food and drink fail to descend, and the diaphragm is stopped up and impassable, the evil qi resides in the stomach duct. If it is in the upper duct, needle to press it down and cause it to descend; if it is in the lower duct, needle to disperse and remove it. When the lower abdomen aches and swells, and urination is obstructed, the evil qi resides in the triple burner; restrain it by selecting the great network vessel of the Taiyang. Observe the network vessels where they knot together with the small network vessels of the Jueyin and contain stagnant blood — if the swelling extends up to the stomach duct, select Zusanli.

To observe the complexion, examine its manner, and know whether the qi is dispersing or gathering again — one observes the color of the eyes, thereby knowing whether the disease persists or has resolved. To unify one's attention on the bodily form and listen for its stirring or stillness — one holds the Qikou (Qi Mouth) pulse and the Renying (Man's Prognosis) pulse to observe the vessels. If the pulse is firm, full, and slippery, the disease is daily advancing; if the pulse is soft, the disease is about to subside; if all the channels are replete, the disease will resolve within three days. The Qikou pulse reflects yin, and the Renying pulse reflects yang.

When evil qi resides in the lung, the disease manifests as pain in the skin, alternating cold and heat, qi rising with panting, sweating, and coughing that moves the shoulders and back. Select the outer transport points of the chest, and the points beside the third vertebra [associated with] the five viscera on the back; press quickly with the hand, and where the patient feels relief, then needle there. Also select the point within the Empty Basin (quepen) to disperse the evil qi outward.

When evil qi resides in the liver, there is pain within both rib-sides, cold in the middle, malign blood lodged within, a tendency for the joints to twitch and pull during movement, and the feet sometimes swell. Select Xingjian (Moving Between) to draw the qi down from beneath the rib-side; supplement Zusanli to warm the middle of the stomach; select the blood vessels to disperse the malign blood; and select the green-blue vessel between the ears to relieve the twitching.

When evil qi resides in the spleen and stomach, the disease manifests as pain in the flesh and muscles. If yang qi is in excess and yin qi is insufficient, there is heat in the middle and a tendency toward hunger; if yang qi is insufficient and yin qi is in excess, there is cold in the middle, intestinal rumbling, and abdominal pain; if yin and yang are both in excess, or both insufficient, there will be both cold and heat — in every case, regulate at Zusanli.

When evil qi resides in the kidney, the disease manifests as pain in the bones, and yin impediment. In yin impediment, pressing on the affected area yields nothing definite; there is abdominal distension, lower back pain, difficulty with bowel movement, pain in the shoulders, back, neck, and nape, and periodic dizziness. Select Yongquan (Bubbling Spring) and Kunlun. Wherever blood appears, remove it entirely.

When evil qi resides in the heart, the disease manifests as heart pain, a tendency toward sorrow, and periodic dizziness and collapse. Observe whether there is excess or deficiency, and regulate accordingly at its transport points.

In skin cold-heat disease, the patient cannot bear to lie against the mat, the body hair is scorched and dry, and the nose is withered and parched. If sweating does not occur, select the network vessels of the three yang, and supplement the Hand Taiyin. In flesh cold-heat disease, there is pain in the flesh, the body hair is scorched, and the lips are withered and parched. If sweating does not occur, select the three yang points in the lower body to remove the stagnant blood, and supplement the Foot Taiyin so as to induce sweating.

In bone cold-heat disease, the patient can find no comfort, and sweat pours out without cease. If the teeth have not yet become withered, select the network vessel of the Shaoyin on the inner thigh; if the teeth have already withered, the condition is fatal and cannot be treated. Bone reversal follows the same rule. In bone impediment, all the joints are unusable and painful, with pouring sweat and vexation in the heart — select the channels of the three yin and supplement them.

When the body has been injured, with much loss of blood, and is also struck by wind and cold, or when one has suffered a fall from a height, so that the four limbs become slack and feeble and cannot be controlled — this is called 'bodily languor' (tiduo). Select the point of the three-fold junction below the navel in the lower abdomen. The three-fold junction is where the Yangming and Taiyin meet, three cun below the navel, at Guanyuan (Gate of the Origin). In reversal-impediment, reversal qi rises up to reach the abdomen. Select the network vessels of yin and yang, observe which governs the disease, and drain the yang channel while supplementing the yin channel.

The pulsating vessel at the side of the neck is Renying (Man's Prognosis). Renying belongs to the Foot Yangming, and lies in front of the 'binding sinew.' Behind the binding sinew is the Hand Yangming, at the point named Futu (Support the Prominence). The next vessel is that of the Foot Shaoyang, at the point named Tianyou (Celestial Window). The next vessel is that of the Foot Taiyang, at the point named Tianzhu (Celestial Pillar). The pulsating vessel beneath the armpit belongs to the Hand Taiyin, at the point named Tianfu (Celestial Storehouse).

For yang-type headache with fullness in the chest and inability to breathe freely, select Renying. For sudden loss of voice with hardened qi in the throat, select Futu and bleed the root of the tongue. For sudden invasion causing clouded qi, with dimness of hearing and sight, select Tianyou. For sudden spasm, seizure, and dizziness, with the legs unable to support the body, select Tianzhu. For sudden impediment with internal rebellion, in which liver and lung contend with each other and blood overflows from the nose and mouth, select Tianfu. These are the five regions of the 'Celestial Window' group.

The Hand Yangming has a branch that enters the cheek and passes among the teeth, at the point named Daying (Great Welcome). For decay of the lower teeth, select this point on the arm; if the patient is averse to cold, supplement it, and if not averse to cold, drain it. The Foot Taiyang has a branch that enters the cheek and passes among the teeth, at the point named Jiaosun (Angle Vertex). For decay of the upper teeth, select the point located in front of the nose and cheek. At the time the disease is active, its pulse will be full — if full, drain it; if deficient, supplement it. One account says to select the point just outside the nose.

The Foot Yangming has a branch that flanks the nose and enters the face, at the point named Xuanlu (Suspended Skull). It connects with the mouth and, opposite, links with the root of the eye. Observe where the fault lies and select accordingly — reduce what is in excess, supplement what is deficient; where the condition is reversed, it will only increase the disorder. The Foot Taiyang has a branch that passes through the nape and enters the brain, directly connecting with the root of the eye, called the 'eye system' (yanxi). For pain and distress in the head and eyes, select the point located between the two sinews in the middle of the nape. Upon entering the brain, this channel separates into the yin qiaomai and yang qiaomai; yin and yang intersect there, with yang entering yin and yin emerging into yang, meeting at the outer canthus of the eye. When yang qi is excessive, the eyes stay wide open; when yin qi is excessive, the eyes close in sleep.

For heat reversal, select the Foot Taiyin and Foot Shaoyang, retaining the needle in both; for cold reversal, select the Foot Yangming and Foot Shaoyin at the foot, retaining the needle in both. For a slack, protruding tongue with drooling saliva and vexed oppression, select the Foot Shaoyin. For shivering with cold, chattering teeth as though struck by a chill, inability to sweat, abdominal distension, and vexed oppression, select the Hand Taiyin. When needling deficiency, needle as the qi is departing; when needling excess, needle as the qi is arriving.

In spring select the network vessels; in summer select the flesh divisions and interstices; in autumn select the Qikou (Qi Mouth); in winter select the channel transport points. In all these, the four seasons each serve as the standard for timing. The network vessels govern the skin, the flesh divisions and interstices govern the muscles, the Qikou governs the sinews and vessels, and the channel transport points govern the bones and marrow. Corresponding to the five viscera, the body has five regions: first, Futu (Crouching Rabbit); second, the calf; third, the back; fourth, the transport points of the five viscera; fifth, the nape. If a welling-abscess or flat-abscess arises in any of these five regions, it is fatal.

When disease begins in the hand or arm, first select the Hand Yangming and Hand Taiyin so as to induce sweating; when disease begins in the head, first select the Taiyang at the nape so as to induce sweating; when disease begins in the foot or shin, first select the Foot Yangming so as to induce sweating. The arm's Taiyin can be made to sweat, and the Foot Yangming can be made to sweat; thus, if selecting a yin point causes excessive sweating, stop it by needling a yang point, and if selecting a yang point causes excessive sweating, stop it by needling a yin point.

Now, the harms of needling are these: if the needle strikes its mark but is not withdrawn, the essence will leak out; if it fails to strike its mark yet is withdrawn, it will only draw evil qi to the site. When essence leaks out, the disease worsens and the patient becomes despondent; when evil qi is drawn in, it gives rise to welling-abscess and flat-abscess.

Where the canthus of the eye opens outward toward the face, this is the outer canthus (the 'sharp canthus'); where it lies to the inside, near the nose, this is the inner canthus. The upper is the outer canthus; the lower is the inner canthus.

When epilepsy (dianji) first begins to arise, the patient first feels unhappy, with heaviness and pain in the head; on looking upward the eyes appear red. When the attack reaches its extreme, and it has passed, the patient feels vexation in the heart. Examine this at the forehead. Select the Hand Taiyang, Yangming, and Taiyin, and stop once the blood [drawn by needling] changes color.

When epilepsy first breaks out, with the mouth drawn taut, crying out, panting, and palpitations, examine the Hand Yangming and Taiyang. If the left side is more rigid, treat the right; if the right side is more rigid, treat the left, and stop once the blood changes color. When epilepsy first breaks out with the body first arching backward rigidly, and consequently pain in the spine, examine the Foot Taiyang, Yangming, Taiyin, and Hand Taiyang, and stop once the blood changes color.

In treating epilepsy, one should remain constantly with the patient, observing the points that ought to be selected. When the disease attacks, observe the site where there is a fault, and drain it; place the blood [drawn] into a gourd vessel — when the next attack occurs, the blood alone will stir. If it does not stir, apply moxibustion twenty times to Qionggu (the 'utmost bone'). Qionggu is the sacrococcygeal bone.

In bone epilepsy, the jaw, the teeth's transport points, and the flesh divisions are all full and swollen, while the bones remain fixed and unmoving, with sweating, vexation and oppression, frequent vomiting of frothy phlegm, and qi leaking downward — this cannot be treated.

In sinew epilepsy, the body is fatigued, with severe contraction and spasm; needle the great channel of the nape at Dazhu (Great Shuttle). If there is frequent vomiting of frothy phlegm and qi leaking downward, this cannot be treated.

In vessel epilepsy, there is sudden collapse, and the vessels of the four limbs are all distended and slack. If the vessels are full, needle all of them to let blood; if not full, apply moxibustion at the Taiyang points flanking the nape, and at the Belt Vessel (dai mai) points three cun apart at the lower back, and at the root transport points of all the flesh divisions. If there is vomiting of frothy phlegm and qi leaking downward, this cannot be treated. In epilepsy, when the attack manifests like mania (kuang), this is fatal and cannot be treated.

When mania first arises, the patient first becomes sorrowful, forgetful, prone to anger, and prone to fear; it is contracted through worry and hunger. Treat by selecting the Hand Taiyang and Yangming, stopping once the blood changes color, and also select the Foot Taiyin and Yangming. When mania first breaks out, the patient sleeps little and does not feel hunger, considers himself lofty and worthy, considers himself eloquent and wise, considers himself honored and noble, and is given to cursing and reviling others without ceasing day or night. Treat by selecting the Hand Yangming, Taiyang, Taiyin, the point beneath the tongue, and the Shaoyin; wherever the pulse is found to be full, select it, and where it is not full, release [leave it untreated].

Manic raving, being easily startled, prone to laughter, fond of singing and merriment, and wandering aimlessly without cease — this is contracted through great fright. Treat by selecting the Hand Yangming, Taiyang, and Taiyin. Mania in which the eyes see falsely, the ears hear falsely, and the patient is prone to shouting — this arises from depleted qi. Treat by selecting the Hand Taiyang, Taiyin, Yangming, the Foot Taiyin, and the two sides of the head at the jaw.

Mania in which the patient eats excessively, is prone to seeing ghosts and spirits, and is prone to laughing without any outward provocation — this is contracted through some occasion of great joy. Treat by selecting the Foot Taiyin, Taiyang, and Yangming, then afterward selecting the Hand Taiyin, Taiyang, and Yangming. When mania is newly arisen and has not yet developed to this degree, first select the pulsating vessels to the left and right of Ququan (Spring at the Bend), and wherever full, let blood; after a short while the condition will resolve. If it does not resolve, treat according to the standard method, and apply moxibustion twenty times to the sacrococcygeal bone.

In wind reversal (fengni), there is sudden swelling of the four limbs, the body feels drenched with sweat, and the patient sighs and shivers with periodic chills; when hungry, there is vexation, and when full, the condition readily changes for the worse. Select the exterior and interior channels of the Hand Taiyin, and the pathways of the Foot Shaoyin and Yangming. If the flesh is cold, select the Spring points; if the bone is cold, select the Well and channel points.

As a disease, reversal-counterflow (jueni) causes the feet to suddenly turn cold, the chest to feel as though it were splitting apart, and the intestines to feel as though cut with a knife; the patient is vexed and cannot eat, and the pulse, whether large or small, is rough and choppy throughout. If there is warmth, select the Foot Shaoyin; if there is cold, select the Foot Yangming — where cold, supplement; where warm, drain. In reversal-counterflow with abdominal distension and fullness, intestinal rumbling, and fullness in the chest with inability to breathe freely, select the points below the chest on both rib-sides — where coughing causes the hand placed there to be moved, along with the back transport points; press with the hand, and the point that brings immediate relief is the correct one.

For internal blockage with inability to urinate, needle the Foot Shaoyin and Taiyang, together with the point reaching up toward the lower abdomen, using a long needle. When qi rebels, select the Taiyin, Yangming, and Jueyin; in severe cases, select the Shaoyin and Yangming — these being the channels where the pulse stirs.

In depleted qi, the body feels drenched with sweat, speech comes in short gasping breaths, the bones ache, the body feels heavy, and the patient is listless and unable to move — supplement the Foot Shaoyin. In shortness of breath, where the breath is short and discontinuous, and exertion exhausts the qi entirely, supplement the Foot Shaoyin, and remove [stagnant blood from] the blood network vessels.
